team.name: Josef Montag
team.bio: Specializes in empirical legal studies and the economics of crime. Currently chairs the Economics Panel at the Czech Science Foundation.

team.name: Michal Šoltés
team.bio: Focuses on empirical criminal law and judicial decision-making. Researcher at IDEA (CERGE-EI).

team.name: Volker Kaul
team.bio: Researches political philosophy and the digital public sphere, with a focus on polarization and democracy.

team.name: Anna Malá
team.bio: Strategic project management and institutional coordination.

team.name: Eva Myšáková
team.bio: Financial oversight, budgeting, and reporting for large-scale European research grants.

team.name: Kateřina Pospíchalová Pavlov
team.bio: Coordinates administrative support, logistics, and institutional communication for the CIOS project.

team.name: Karolína Martínek
team.bio: Responsible for data management planning and ensuring open access to research outputs.

team.name: Alice Dvořáková
team.affiliation: Prague University of Economics and Business
team.bio: Research interests include macroeconomic links between financial systems, technological innovation, and wealth distribution.

team.name: Petr Novák
team.bio: Focuses on consumer protection law and behavioral impacts in online digital marketplaces.

team.name: Sarah Jenkins
team.bio: Applies natural language processing (NLP) to historical court records to identify systemic linguistic bias.

team.name: Tomáš Kučera
team.bio: Investigating algorithmic bias and experimental nudges in social media feeds.

team.name: Anna Louisa Bindler
team.bio: Leading expertise in empirical legal studies and the economics of crime.

team.name: Susann Fiedler
team.bio: Prominent researcher in behavioral economics and psychology.

team.name: Barbara Havelková
team.bio: Specialist in gender legal studies, equality law, and comparative legal systems.

team.name: Elena Kantorowicz-Reznichenko
team.role: Professor of Quantitative Empirical Legal Studies
team.bio: Expert in the economic analysis of law and criminal justice systems.

team.name: Keren Weinshall
team.bio: Empirical researcher focusing on judicial decision-making and public law.

publications.title: The Impact of Algorithmic Support on Judicial Decision Making
publications.abstract: This paper evaluates the rollout of the PRECID algorithmic support system among Czech judges. Using a difference-in-differences approach, we find that access to predictive sentencing guidelines reduces unwarranted disparities in sentencing outcomes by 14% without altering the overall severity of punishments. The findings suggest that data-driven tools can effectively guide human discretion in the criminal justice system.

publications.title: Understanding Sentencing Disparities in the Digital Age
publications.abstract: In this study, we analyze the impact of digital transformation on judicial sentencing consistency. By leveraging a longitudinal dataset of over 50,000 cases, we identify how information availability correlates with sentencing variance across different jurisdictions.

publications.title: Digital Governance and Rule of Law: A European Perspective
publications.abstract: This chapter explores the evolving landscape of digital governance within the European Union. It examines how automated decision-making systems intersect with fundamental rights and the traditional rule of law frameworks, proposing a new regulatory paradigm for algorithmic accountability.

publications.title: Digital Public Sphere and Societal Polarization
publications.abstract: We examine how algorithmically curated social media environments affect political polarization. Through a series of behavioral experiments, we demonstrate that exposure to opposing viewpoints in a highly digitized public sphere often backfires, increasing affective polarization rather than mitigating it.

publications.title: Mapping Bias: NLP Applications in Historic Case Law
publications.abstract: Applying machine learning to a century of appellate court records, we map the evolution of linguistic bias in sentencing rationales, demonstrating a persistent but shifting disparity in demographic treatment based on geographic jurisdiction.

publications.title: Escaping the Echo Chamber: Experimental Evidence from Social Media
publications.abstract: Through a randomized controlled trial involving 2,000 users, we test whether algorithmic nudges out of political echo chambers reduce or exacerbate affective polarization. We find that brief exposure to counter-narratives decreases polarization, but sustained exposure triggers a backfire effect.
